questions.stem: In GNUplot, what does the command `plot sin(x)` do?
options: Draws a graph of the function `sin(x)`
Prints the value of `sin(x)` at one point
questions.explanation: `plot` is GNUplot's main command for drawing graphs. Given a function such as `sin(x)`, `plot sin(x)` draws its curve over the current x range. It graphs the function rather than printing a single value.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: How do you plot the data contained in a file called `data.dat` in GNUplot?
questions.explanation: To plot data from a file, you give its name in quotes, as in `plot "data.dat"`. GNUplot then reads the columns of the file and plots them. The file name must be quoted so it is treated as a string.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does the clause `using 1:2` mean in `plot "data.dat" using 1:2` ?
options: Use rows 1 to 2 of the file
Plot column 1 on the x axis against column 2 on the y axis
Plot only the first two points
questions.explanation: The `using` clause selects which columns to plot. `using 1:2` means take column 1 as the x value and column 2 as the y value for each row. It is how you choose the columns from a multi-column data file.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which command sets the range of the x axis to run from 0 to 10 in GNUplot?
questions.explanation: Axis ranges are set with `set xrange` and square brackets, so `set xrange [0:10]` makes the x axis span from 0 to 10. The y axis is controlled similarly with `set yrange`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does `plot [0:6] sin(x)` do with the part `[0:6]` ?
options: Plots `sin(x)` only for x between 0 and 6
questions.explanation: An inline range in square brackets right after `plot` limits the x values used for this plot, so `plot [0:6] sin(x)` draws the sine curve only from x equal to 0 up to 6. It is a quick alternative to a separate `set xrange`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which option draws a data series as a connected line rather than separate points?
questions.explanation: Adding `with lines` to a plot command joins the data with a continuous line, as in `plot "data.dat" with lines`. `with points` would instead mark each data point separately without connecting them.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does the style `with linespoints` do?
options: Draws both a connecting line and a marker at each point
questions.explanation: `with linespoints` combines the two styles: it draws a line through the data and also places a marker at each data point. It is useful when you want to see both the trend and the individual measurements.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which pair of commands directs GNUplot output into a PNG image file instead of the screen?
questions.explanation: You first choose an output format with `set terminal png`, then name the file with `set output "fig.png"`. The next `plot` writes the graph into that PNG file rather than displaying it on screen.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What is the role of `set terminal` in GNUplot?
options: It sets the title of the plot
It selects the output format or device, such as the screen or a PNG file
questions.explanation: `set terminal` chooses where and in what format the plot is produced, for example an interactive window or a `png`, `pdf` or `postscript` file. It is paired with `set output` when writing to a file.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which command gives the whole plot a title reading `My Graph` ?
questions.explanation: `set title "My Graph"` sets the heading shown above the plot. The text is given in quotes. (Note that a `title` keyword inside a `plot` command instead labels a single curve in the legend, not the whole figure.)
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which command labels the x axis with the text `Time (s)` ?
questions.explanation: Axis labels are set with `set xlabel` and `set ylabel`. So `set xlabel "Time (s)"` writes that text beneath the x axis. The text is quoted as a string.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: By default, how does GNUplot draw data read from a file when no style is given?
questions.explanation: When you plot a data file without specifying a style, GNUplot draws the data as separate points by default. To connect them you must add `with lines` (or `with linespoints`).
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: How can you plot two curves, `sin(x)` and `cos(x)`, on the same axes in one command?
questions.explanation: You list several plot elements separated by commas, so `plot sin(x), cos(x)` draws both curves together on the same axes. Using `+` would instead plot the single function that is their sum.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does the `replot` command do in GNUplot?
options: Redraws the last plot, including any new settings or added curves
Saves the plot to a file
questions.explanation: `replot` repeats the previous `plot` command, picking up any settings you changed in between (such as a new title or range) and any extra curve you append. It is handy for refining a graph step by step.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which character begins a comment in a GNUplot script?
questions.explanation: GNUplot, like the shell and Python, treats text after a `#` on a line as a comment that is ignored. This lets you annotate your plotting scripts.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which command turns on a grid in the background of the plot?
questions.explanation: `set grid` switches on grid lines aligned with the axis tick marks, making values easier to read off. It can be turned off again with `unset grid`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: After writing to a file, why is `set output` important when using a file terminal such as PNG?
options: It chooses the colour of the line
It names the file the plot is written to and finalises it
questions.explanation: When the terminal is a file format like PNG, `set output "fig.png"` names the destination file. Closing the output (for example with `set output` again or `unset output`) ensures the file is properly written. Without it, GNUplot would not know where to send the image.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which command would plot the function `x**2` (x squared) in GNUplot?
questions.explanation: GNUplot uses `**` for exponentiation, so `plot x**2` draws the parabola for x squared. The drawing command is always `plot` for 2D graphs, not `graph` or `draw`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does `set yrange [-1:1]` do?
options: Sets the x axis from -1 to 1
Sets the y axis to run from -1 to 1
questions.explanation: `set yrange [-1:1]` fixes the vertical axis to span from -1 to 1, which is convenient for functions like `sin(x)` whose values stay within that range. The x axis would be controlled by `set xrange`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: In `plot "data.dat" using 1:3`, which columns are used?
options: Column 1 for x and column 3 for y
Column 3 for x and column 1 for y
questions.explanation: The `using` clause is read as x:y, so `using 1:3` takes column 1 as the x coordinate and column 3 as the y coordinate, skipping column 2. It lets you pick any two columns to plot against each other.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which command gives a single curve the legend label `Speed` ?
questions.explanation: A `title` keyword inside a `plot` command labels that particular curve in the legend (the key). So `plot "data.dat" title "Speed"` shows `Speed` in the legend for that data. `set xlabel` would instead label the axis.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which command is used to draw a three-dimensional surface plot in GNUplot?
questions.explanation: `splot` (surface plot) is GNUplot's command for 3D graphs of functions of two variables or three-column data, for example `splot sin(x)*cos(y)`. The ordinary `plot` command is only for 2D graphs.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does `unset key` do to a GNUplot graph?
options: Removes the legend (key) from the plot
questions.explanation: The key is the legend that names each curve. `unset key` removes that legend box, which is useful when a single unlabelled curve does not need one. To bring it back you would use `set key`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which command would plot the data file `temps.dat` joined up as a line?
questions.explanation: You name the file in quotes and add the style, so `plot "temps.dat" with lines` reads the file and draws its points connected by a line. The keyword is `with lines`, and the file name must be quoted.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Complete these basic GNUplot commands.
questions.solution: To draw a graph of the function sine of x, you type {{1}}. To plot the data stored in a file named `data.dat`, you type {{2}}.
questions.explanation: `plot` is the drawing command for both functions and data files; the file name must be quoted, giving `plot sin(x)` and `plot "data.dat"`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Choose the right command to set each axis range.
questions.solution: To make the x axis run from 0 to 10, you use {{1}}. To make the y axis run from -1 to 1, you use {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Ranges use `set xrange` and `set yrange` with square brackets, so `set xrange [0:10]` and `set yrange [-1:1]` set the two axes.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Pick the plotting style for each description.
questions.solution: To connect the data points with a continuous line, add {{1}}. To draw both a line and a marker at each point, add {{2}}.
questions.explanation: `with lines` draws a connected line, while `with linespoints` draws both the line and a marker at each data point.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Complete the steps to save a plot to a PNG file.
questions.solution: First you choose the output format with {{1}}. Then you name the destination file with {{2}}, and finally you run your `plot` command.
questions.explanation: `set terminal png` selects the PNG format and `set output "fig.png"` names the file; the following `plot` then writes the image to that file.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Choose the command that labels the plot and an axis.
questions.solution: To set the overall title of the graph to `Results`, you use {{1}}. To label the x axis as `Time`, you use {{2}}.
questions.explanation: `set title` sets the heading of the whole figure and `set xlabel` labels the x axis, so the two commands are `set title "Results"` and `set xlabel "Time"`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Reason about plotting columns and two functions together.
questions.solution: To plot column 1 against column 2 of `data.dat`, you add the clause {{1}}. To draw both `sin(x)` and `cos(x)` on the same axes in one command, you separate them with {{2}}.

options: a comma, as in `plot sin(x), cos(x)`
a plus, as in `plot sin(x) + cos(x)`
a pipe, as in `plot sin(x) | cos(x)`
gaps.answer: a comma, as in `plot sin(x), cos(x)`

questions.explanation: `using 1:2` reads as x:y, plotting column 1 against column 2, and separate plot elements are listed with commas, so `plot sin(x), cos(x)` draws both curves together.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)
